author: @TimeSurfer206
text:
My friends who live off grid have a simple trick for anything and everything that has a "Standby" mode, or a transformer of any type, especially all the damn chargers that breed worse than rabbits.
They plug these items into a Plug Strip, usually surge protected, with a switch. It's cheaper to have all the different chargers on when you're charging just one, than to have them all on all the time.
My own biggest instant saving was to shoot my clothes dryer. We used our clothes line. We were running average 3 loads a day.
An hour a load, roughly, at a nice middle of the road 3,000 Watts, is 9KWh a day, 270KWh a month (Now you see why I used THREE!), which, at $0.1184 US per KWh is a monthly cost of $32.00 at close enough ($31.968 for the pedantic people in the crowd). I have installed clotheslines in my basement, garage, and even living room, just to not feed a dryer. And the clothes last longer.
I currently proudly live in a Caravan, the original "Tiny house." Currently, Air Conditioning is my biggest expense. So, I am putting up sunshades. These things are an Easy-Bake Oven when the sun hits them. It is making a huge difference.

text:
I have a solar powered box truck cooler.  During the day it's self sufficient.  I unplug it from 6am to 7pm.  We cook more in the winter less in the summer. If you go this route probably doing dishes are free.  Hot water is almost ready for tea or coffee before it hits the stove.

Waste heat in an attic could heat hot water as could waste heat from the air conditioner.  If I was really cheap I'd use a no drip/condensation air conditioner and turn it around in the winter to use as a heat pump.  Floor fans pick cold air up from the ground and feel like Air conditioning, there's a bid difference between floor temps and ceiling temps.
Infrared heat things not the air kinda like standing in the sun or in the shade.  One 400 watt infrared heater feels more comfortable than a 1500 watt fan forced heater.

author: @justinw1765
text:
Quick point about using fans vs AC.  If you live in a lower to moderate humidity climate, fans would be ok, but an HVAC guy pointed out to me that fans create a pressure differential in a house which has the effect to pull in more humidity from the outside, which for a high humidity climate like the one I live at, is not a good thing. Half the battle of cooling here, is taking out humidity from the air. 

     A small fan, on low setting, and close to you will not be so much an issue, but larger fans on higher speeds can/will be an issue in this regard.

author: @deadduck8307
text: 99% great advice. @ 5:20 though you say to get a smart plug if you charge your phone overnight?  I actually did look into this several months back but found that true 'smart' plugs run a MIPS processor (same as a cheap router) for the WiFi connectivity and GPIO pins so they take about 2-5 watts continuously. Dumb analog timers use about 1-2 watts to run the mechanical bits, dumb digital timers use about 0.5/1 watt (with an LCD w/ no back light). I find the charger itself takes microamps while not in use... To each their own, but I highly advice just leaving it plugged in if you must -- the only savings to be had is to manually unplug it.

author: @MrChris20912
text:
Awareness is Primary.

Vote &  Participate in local initiatives - Vote for people who are going to put climate action as a first priority. When done right, it creates jobs and new industries.

Until we get governments and companies to take notice and work to improve power production and distribution, below are the things we can do, as Robert describes in the video, to mitigate the impact to our wallets and that are currently in our control - when the power companies raise their rates. 

Robert's List:
1) Power Vampires/Phantom Load - have an off switch, such as when you have any electronics plugged in, especially adapter, turn them off at the plug strip.inventory
2) Fridge - keep it full, of food or bottled water. The more cold mass in the fridge, the more stable the temp and less it has to work to maintain if you are opening it often.
3) Radiators - don't cover or block them with furniture.
4) Dishwasher - Fill it properly and only run it when full.
5) Hob Ring/Electric stove coil - Use a burner appropriate to the size of the pot. Keep it clean for better heat transfer (and less smoke).
6) Microwave - Use it more - For small meals the microwave will use less energy. Slow cookers can also be useful as lower energy use cookers. "Air Fryers" are small forced air counter top cookers that can also be more efficient than using a large oven to warm a single meal (though not as handy for a full size turkey or ham.) There are also some amazing toaster ovens that are more oven than toaster, which can cut down on cooking power usage. 
7) Showers - Shower rather than a full bath. Also, set a timer or turn off the water while lathering up. I remember years ago in London, having a student flat where it cost me 20p for 3 minutes of warmish shower water.
8) Curtains - Closed at night in the winter to retain heat, and closed in the day time to keep out the heat in summer. 
9) Phone Charging - Unplug before bed, or use a smart charger. (see #1 above)
10) TV - Buy a more efficient TV. (This should apply to most all electronics). In the USA, the average power consumption per year is usually shown on new TVs in the stores. 

11) Ceiling fans for circulating air in spaces without central HVAC/air conduits (or even when they do, but the circulation sucks) More often seen in southern climates, but that heat is moving North and these are much less costly to run than air conditioning units.
12) Heat Pump HVAC units - If you do own a home and are looking at heating and cooling options - Heat Pump HVAC units are an increasingly affordable solution - especially with growing State and Federal incentives for high efficiency home upgrades increasingly becoming available.

author: @scsft
text:
Here's one of my favorite tricks:
Get a digital timer outlet or remote controlled outlet with timer functionality and set it to power off at 2:00 a.m. or some other time at which you are certainly sleeping.
If you want to use the device, you manually have to turn the timer on and the device will stay on until you turn it off again.
But if you forget it, it will be turned off automatically so that it can use at most one day worth of electricity in standby mode.

author: @debbiehenri345
text:
My family started cutting down on our electricity in April, during the first round of price increases.

Firstly, it calls for me to spend an inordinate amount of time switching off lights/things everyone else leaves burning. 
(I do get told off for nagging if I point things out lights being left on. So I realised that if I just get on with it and then show the reduced bill afterwards with a sort of 'See? Told you so' attitude, this actually makes a difference).

We turned off the immersion heater. It's one of the most ridiculous energy eating devices in the house. Someone has a wash, washes up breakfast things, and the tank fills up with more cold water turning the rest of it tepid. What a waste of energy.
So we boil only what we absolutely need in the kettle. 
I can wash in cold water very happily (I'm a cold-loving person and dislike it when the heating goes on in winter). 
We're vegans/vegetarians, and we've had no issue with washing up most of our dishes and kitchen utensils in cold water. No meat juices, greases, blood etc needing hot water.

We each have one 4 minute shower per week. (We keep a clock on a shelf opposite the shower so we know how long we take). 
We wash at the basin on all other days. 
My hair is rather on the greasy side, so I just tie it up in a headscarf until shower day. No one cares. 

We had 3 dehumidifiers running nearly all the time, as we have a Scottish hillside cottage in one of Britain's wettest regions. They're probably one of the biggest contributors to our bills. 
So I bought some fine insect mesh, husband made some frames out of dumped wooden floorboards to go over selected windows, and we can now air the entire cottage all day without the place filling up with millions of insects.
We won't be able to do this so much during the winter, unfortunately, but we can still air the place on any warmer, drier days. 

We got rid of the lawnmower 20 years ago. 
I let my lawn grown into a meadow, added lots of different wild flowers to it - and it has become a haven for wildlife. 
No one needs to cut their lawn, it is one of the most unnatural features in any garden and was a habit started by ordinary citizens because they wanted to copy the rich. Now, considering the vast lawns of somewhere like Buckingham Palace, the Royal Parks, etc - that 5 x 5 ft manicured square of sheared green desert out the front starts to look a bit silly in comparison. So, return to the cottage garden style, grow flowers that attract wildlife, herbs that flavour your food, and a few veggies to reduce your grocery bills. 
Even if you grow just 3-5 courgette plants, you will have so many veg off those few plants in a summer, you won't be buying anything from the supermarket. 

We have started to use a barbeque (using wood, not charcoal). 
I never had one before, and found that it does not take long at all to cook food or heat water. It's a habit I'm still trying to get into at the moment.
I have a multi fuel-burning stove. We grow our own trees for this and there's plenty more wood laying around locally. Our previous stove was linked up to radiators, but was not an efficient system. So when the old water jacketed stove burst one day, creating a massive mess, we went with a solid type and just let the heat permeate the cottage (2-bed, so it's a small place that doesn't need much heat). It's very good, very efficient. 

Cold food - yes, I reduced the amount of hot food we eat all this summer (to reduce the gas bills),  and have kept our shopping bills down to £40 per week (for 3 of us) by growing more stuff. I haven't bought any fruit all summer - due to the number of blackcurrant, raspberry, and many other fruit bushes I bought and planted into our meadow. 
I've noticed that since we went vegan our calor gas bottles for running the oven have lasted much longer anyway. I suppose because we were no longer roasting chickens of a weekend, we saved a lot of gas. 

Husband swaps his laptop for a smaller phone-like device when he wants to listen to a debate or an audiobook on YouTube.
I got my son to go onto the 'settings' bit on my laptop and change the 'skin' colour of some of the pages, swapping the standard, glaring 'white' page (which must need more electricity) for a black one instead. And I have to say, the black background on Google and YouTube is much, much gentler on the eyes. 

As well as wearing warmer clothes and cuddling up under throws during colder weather, we also wear hats indoors. 
We all lose something like 80% of our heat through our heads, so if you wear a hat and make sure grandma and grandad do the same, that's going to save a lot of personal heat loss.

replies.author: @justinw1765
replies.text:
@harpbloke     Even better are vacuum insulated glass panels, which one can make at home.  Get some good glass (a little wider than your window glass), use a vacuum rated epoxy, and affix wooden frames around same to one glass pane. Then tape aluminum tape on the outside and inside of the wooden frame. Cut a piece of bamboo to the same height as the wooden frame. Drill two small holes in the side of the bamboo column. Affix that into the center of the glass pane with with the vacuum rated epoxy. Then affix the other glass pane onto the wooden frame/bamboo column.  Let it all set for the necessary time.  Then drill a hole into the middle of one of the wooden frames, line said hole with some of that vacuum rated epoxy, and once that sets, pull a light'ish vacuum onto it (around 28" Hg would be good) and seal it up. (You can even use something like a "Pump N Seal" system with the tabs--just make sure that you put some aluminum tape over the sealing tab after you pulled the vacuum on there. Obviously the wooden frame will need to be wide enough to put the pump sealer on).

    You now have like an R20 or so (depending on how much space between the glass panes) window add on---far, far, far much more insulation than bubblewrap and also clearer.  Put industrial strength hook and loop or velcro on the corners of the vacuum insulation panel and matching ones one the frame of your windows (obviously do all this on the inside of the house, so the vacuum insulation panel is not exposed to the weather AND it doesn't look super funky to people looking at your house).

author: @Patriarchtech
text: This requires some investment. But if you can get yourself a hybrid inverter and say a 5-10kwh battery pack there is some potential for savings. If your utility provider has variable rates for kwh costs depending on the time of the day for instance(most do in Japan and Denmark anyway) then you can use the grid to charge your battery at night at 30% off and then use your battery during daytime or when the electricity is expensive. These inverters are very versatile and you don't really need  solar panels. There are efficiency losses of 10-15% or so in converting back and forth between ac and DC, but it is relatively simple to do. You simply add a smart plug to the ac input of the inverter.

If you use your tumble drier on a sense mode it will switch off when the clothes are dry, not carry on until the time runs out. In that case it will not matter how much you load it up below optimum. In fact the lack of free space for the clothes to tumble in will increase the drying time!

I do feel I need to say a word about blame for this price hike ...... the Suppliers (the ones you pay your bill to) are not the real villains here. The real villains are the Energy Producers. They charge a rate for the gas they sell and demand is high (Putin cutting his supply to Europe is making this all vastly worse than it should be) prices go up. If Europe were not dependent on Russian gas this would not be a thing .... but a couple of years ago they laughed in the face of the Orange One when he pointed this out. So blame is both Geopolitical machinations AND Producers taking advantage.
To be clear there are others making hay over this. Rishi Sunak still gets his Vat on the total price of fuel and as that is a % he has double the tax revenue he had and will get double that again by next year! Remember that when they talk about "giving" you £400! The Government has the power to do more than it has. This is the case in Europe where the energy price hike has been far less than here!

Bes energy saving tips I can give are ..... Get Solar (if you have £10K to spend). Unplug stuff you are not using. Only fill the kettle with enough water to fill your cup. Turn the thermostat down a bit. Don't be afraid of  dark house. If you don't have a smart meter (smart move) get a clip on meter that will show you exactly how much power you are using. Install a PIV unit to suck hot air in and expel the cold air from your home (gets rid of damp and mould too) Make sure your boiler is set to low flow temp.

author: @gameoverwehaveeverypixelco1258
text:
Happening here in Australia too but not by that much. 
Heres some energy saving ideas I use. 
- when you boil water in kettle put excess hot water in a flask to stay hot for use later.
- buy camping fold out solar panels with USB charging to charge up battery banks to use for lighting or charge phone. I charge my phone from solar charged power bank, I have USB lights that I use to light up my room. 
- air dry clothes.
- use a camping Solar water heater bag and use water to wash dishes or even have a warm shower, bring inside to shower. 
- make paper logs to cook meals or Boil water. Paper logs just newspapers rolled up and covered in used cooking oil, stored in a plastic tub. Can make a basic brick bbq with an old wire grill, cook whatever you can using this to save power. 
-buy cheap solar lights to bring inside for lighting. Combine a few together and make a lamp, just leave outside everyday and bring inside at night. 
- you can buy a solar shed light with a sensor on it. We have one in bathroom,solar panel runs outside window to charge and at night we walk into bathroom and it automatically turns on. 
You can also buy battery operated sensor lights for inside that you can use too in bathroom or kitchen. Or push lights. Batteries can be rechargable AA OR AAA which can be recharged using the fold out camping Solar panel and USB battery charger. Mine recharges two batteries at once. 
- play movies on a portable DVD player instead of a large tv or watch movies on your phone or tablet charged by solar recharged battery banks.

author: @rogerstarkey5390
text:
Here's one.
Internet (/TV)
I had a contract with a well known provider.
Basic internet (30Gb?)
"Basic" (ish) TV
Perfectly adequate for my needs (and probably most users)
The cisyt was gradually creeping up from £30 at one point, towards £80(😱😱)
.
So I audited what I actually USED it for.
I used the service (surprisingly) when I was indoors..... 👍
.
Basic information.
.
"Some" TV (decreasing)
.
Social media.
.
Watching Quality YouTube context 😉
.......
5GHz WiFi was coming.
I had a good mobile contract.
£20, unlimited everything.
🤔🤔🤔
Searched for a 5G mobile.
,
Gambled on a Chinese one for £220.
Bought myself:-
A 5G Bluetooth WiFi dongle for the PC (£5)
.
An Amazon firestick (£20) (plugged into my TV)
.
Cancelled the fibre contract (they didn't like that!)
.
Everything goes through my 5Ghz phone hotspot! (Unlimited data!)
.
I now have BETTER internet than before (always way above 100Mbps) through my phone, PC and TV.
.
All but one of the TV channels I used to watch. (Oh well)
.
The £80  monthly saving paid for the phone and other stuff in 3 months.
.
Oh! I renegotiated the phone contract DOWN to £16 for unlimited calls, Data and messaging.
.....
Next?
My (old) LCD TV takes 240W when running (!!)
.
Latest similar models, NUCH better quality, use about 70-80W.
I can buy a "Cheap" (but MUCH  BETTER) TV and it will pay for itself in about 6-9 months based on my usage and the upcoming increases.
.
That's me off to a well known retailer for a look!

author: @robertgaines-tulsa
text: I've never had radiators, so I've never been in a situation where furniture got pushed up against them. I'd suggest using lengths of 2x4 (lumber) as spacers behind the feet of a sofa and a wall to ensure that space is always maintained. Yeah, I'm guilty of constantly charging my phone. I store it upright in a charge cradle so I can quickly get to it. The electricity cost in Oklahoma is pretty low, so I've never worried about it. I could put it on a timer if I got worried. I almost never leave the house, so the portability of it is almost unwanted. I wish I had a heat pump dryer and water heater. I've never heard of a condenser dryer. Just gas and resistive heat other than the newer heat pump. I never heard of a stove top being called a hob before. We got stuck with resistive electric heat after our gas furnace broke down. It costs too much to put in a new gas furnace or a heat pump system, so I cringe about higher energy prices. Oddly enough, the air conditioning in the summer costs a little more than the resistive electric heating in the winter. It could be due to global warming. Winter used to be colder than it is now.

replies.author: @deslomeslager
replies.text: If it freezes outside you can take something outside (a 2L bottle with water) and let it freeze and put it in your fridge. Put it high, as cold air goes down. If it only freezes at night this still works. Another thing is to keep the freezer compartment clean. As soon as ice deposits on the curly tubes, they will prevent the freezer from cooling very well (ice is harder to penetrate than air). Last tip: If your fridge is not an A+++ version (very low power rating that is), buy a new one with the best rating. It may have a much better insulation and a compressor that is far more efficient.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: nice advice from deslomeslager mate

replies.author: @spex357
replies.text:
​@deslomeslager 
As my fridge was over thirty years old I recently changed it for a new one with new insulation and new door seals. If it saves me money so be it, it's still too expensive.
If it's not cold enough outside to freeze water then a few days is all frozen food will last without energy. If power cuts are going to be the norm and the price is going to rise to thousands per year, then freezers might be for the chop. 
I come from a time when there was no freezers, and the mentality of the 1930's depression could still be seen. Folk stored tinned stuff like Pineapple rings, Fruit salad, tinned ham, Spam, and Fray Bentos pies, which could also be used as currency, on the street. 
We have been in a recession in the UK since the 1st of January, but the government is still not admitting it, by the time they do we will be in a depression with the rest of the west, and a tin of Spam might be worth a gallon of dodgy petrol.

author: @edwardvanhazendonk
text: I have big savings after rinsing my radiators internally (lot's of debry collects in it including iron), changed the radiator valves to thermostatic and controllable via Honeywell Evohome (bought it secondhand). This brought down my gas usage from around 800m3 to a bit north of 400m3. (In the Netherlands this saving could give you around a €1000 a year). Also installed a radiator valve on the shower, installed LED lights, heat only rooms I'm in and switch heating to low when away (Evohome helps with this). Also looking at your energy consumption in depth would give you hints about your usage, which helps you save a lot. When you understand Dutch (or if youtube translates it well) you kan watch channel Ketel Klets with all kinds of savings. (For instance lowering your radiator temperatures to 55 degrees saves a lot of power heating the water, placing a temperature controlled fan under it heats your room a lot faster. Thanks for sharing Robert!

author: @ddbb6618
text: Use a thermos (insulated flask) for left over hot water after boiling a kettle.  Easier than trying to boil just the right amount of water for a cup.of tea or coffee.

replies.author: @simonduffy99
replies.text: When I make instant coffee, I measure out exactly the right amount of water and only heat it up enough to make it a "hot" drink. Water requires quite a bit of energy to bring it all the way up to boiling point. Clearly, if your making tea or brewing fresh coffee, that wouldn't work. Either way, just heat the exact amount you need, then there's nothing to put in your thermos :-)

replies.author: @jochenschrey2909
replies.text: I 've never seen any thermos small enough to look efficient for half or a quarter of a cup of surplus boiling water. I recommend owning twonelectric kettles; one full size family monster handling 2l for those occasions when you want to make tea for the whole day and since those frequently demand a 0.5l minimum filling far more importantly a smaller "travel" kettle that would handle less to heat just your cup of luke warm instant coffee. I am using the latter all the time. If you are transfering tap water into your kettle maybe get a transparent vessel and mark its outside boldly with sticky tape, to get just your mug of water.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: nice!

replies.author: @ddbb6618
replies.text: @jochenschrey2909 Ok  yes I agree only boiling what is required is the most efficient. Though if you have limited power and using solid fuel stove or similar  the thermos flask has merits.  Other than that I agree boil what you need.
